div.div:
Series:
- 1D array of indexed data
- NumPy array has an implicitly defined integer index while `Series` has an explicitly defined index associated with the values
- Think Series as dictionary - a structure that maps typed keys to a set of typed values
- Indices even can be noncontiguous or nonsequential indices. e.g. `index=[2, 5, 3, 7]`

div.div:
DataFrame:
- 2D array with row INDICES and column NAME
- Think DataFrame as dictionary (same as Series) - maps a column name to a Series of column data
- In a two-dimensional NumPy array, data[0] will return the first row. For a DataFrame, data['col0'] will return the first
column. Because of this, it is probably better to think about DataFrames as generalized dictionaries

div.div:
Index:
- It is an immutable array
- It is a set. e.g. `indA.intersection(indB)` or `indA & indB`
- Index object may contain repeated values. Repeated indices are valid but the outcome is often undesirable
- Indices are PRESERVED during computation of Series/DataFrame

div.div:
Slicing Series (Same applies for DataFrame):
- Explicit index when slicing (data['a':'c']) - final index ('c') is included
- Impli
cit index when slicing (data[0:2]) - final index (2) is excluded

div.span: Missing data are refered as null, NaN, NA values
- Unlike Numpy, Pandas convert None to NaN (if dtype != object)

span.style: color: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(237, 237, 237)); text-align: center; background-color: transparent;

div.span: np.array([1, np.nan, 2, None]) # array([1, nan, 2, None], dtype=object)
- pd.Series([1, np.nan, 2, None]) # ser[3]=NaN, ser.dtype= float64

switch.text: General Rule: Missing data are refered as null, NaN, NA values...

- Con: Adds overhead in both storage and computation

div.div: - Use a sentinel (gaurd) value to indicate a missing value
- e.g. -9999 for int values. 'NaN' for floating values, 'None' for object typed values (slow performance, AVOID IT)

div.div: Hierarchical/Multi Indexing

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 618px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1320px; margin-left: 172px;

div.div:
Usage:
- Allows to store higher-dimensional data in Series (1D) and DataFrame (2D)
-
Each extra level in a multi-index represents an extra dimension
- Pandas provide 3D `Panel` and 4D `Panel4D` (Less popular)

switch.text: Usage:- Allows to store higher-dimensional data in Series (1D) and DataFrame (2D)...

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 618px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1395px; margin-left: 172px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: "Helvetica"; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.div:
- Think MultiIndex as array of tuple: [('California', 2000), ('California', 2010), ... ('Texas', 2010)]
- MultiIndex(levels=[['California', 'New York', 'Texas'], [2000, 2010]], labels=[[0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2], [0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1]])
- '0' in labels[0] represents California; '0' in labels[1] represents 2000

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 618px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1545px; margin-left: 172px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: "Helvetica"; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.div:
- It allows indexing just one of the levels in the index. The result is same object, with the lower-level indices maintained
- Partial slicing is available as well, as long as the MultiIndex is SORTED

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 618px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1470px; margin-left: 172px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: "Helvetica"; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
Thumb Rule for Slicing:
- Many of the MultiIndex slicing operations will fail if the index is not sorted.
-
Indexing and slicing on a MultiIndex is designed to be intuitive, and it helps if you think about the indices as added dimensions

div.div: Joins

rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 618px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1692px; margin-left: 172px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: "Helvetica"; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
- Similar to Database join
- Categories:
- one-to-one: key column do not contain duplicate entries. Very similar to column concatenation.
- many-to-one: one of the two key columns contains duplicate entries. Resulting DataFrame will preserve those duplicate entries as appropriate.
- many-to-many: Both key columns contains duplicate entries. Similar to DB CROSS JOIN.
- NOTE: merge in general discards the index, except in the special case of merges by index

a.text: Text is not SVG - cannot display
